title: Serre’s Finiteness Theorem (Homotopy Groups of Spheres Part II)
text:
It’s about time I put a bow on the previous blog post about computing homotopy groups of spheres and discussed the proof of Serre’s finiteness theorem:
My approach to this is a natural continuation of the methods I used to compute \(\pi_4(S^3)\) in my last post. I’m not doing anything especially clever or fancy; it’s honestly more of a sequence of brute-force computations. Pretty much all the cohomology computations I state use the same process with the Serre spectral sequence that my computation of \(\pi_4(S^3)\) employed, so I will omit those details in the interest of everyone’s sanity.

title: Serre’s Finiteness Theorem (Homotopy Groups of Spheres Part II)
text:
Rational Homotopy Theory
To start off, we can note a couple things:

We know that \(\pi_k(S^n) = 0\) for \(k < n\) and \(\pi_n(S^n) \simeq \mathbb{Z}\) so we only need to focus on the case that \(k > n\) for Serre’s theorem.
For \(n = 0,1\) we can completely compute all homotopy groups and see that they vanish except for \(k = n\), so we can focus on \(n > 1\) (which in particular means \(S^n\) will always be simply connected).
For \(n = 2\) the Hopf map induces isomorphisms \(\pi_k(S^3) \xrightarrow{\sim} \pi_k(S^2)\) for all \(k \geq 3\); hence we can focus on the case where \(n \geq 3\).

Last time, I sketched a proof using the homological Serre spectral sequence that the homotopy groups of a finite simply-connected CW complex (e.g. \(S^n\) for \(n > 1\)) are finitely generated. In light of this fact, we observe that Serre’s finiteness theorem is equivalent to the following:

Theorem 2 (Serre, rational version) \(\pi_k(S^n) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Q}= 0\), except in two cases:

If \(k = n\) then \(\pi_n(S^n) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Q}\simeq \mathbb{Q}\);
If \(n\) is even then \(\pi_{2n-1}(S^n) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Q}\simeq \mathbb{Q}\).


This is a nicer statement to try to prove, since it is now a precise computation of the so-called “rational homotopy groups.” On the other hand, we cannot immediately start computing these groups without first establishing some tools; fortunately, we can fairly easily modify our existing set of tools for computing homotopy groups to work in this new rational setting.
Recall that the go-to method for computing homotopy groups was to convert them into homology groups using the Whitehead tower and the Hurewicz theorem. We can still do this trick – since \(\mathbb{Q}\) is flat as a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module, the universal coefficient theorem tells us that \[H_k(X; \mathbb{Q}) \simeq (H_k(X; \mathbb{Z}) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Q}) \oplus \operatorname{Tor}_1^{\mathbb{Z}}(H_{k-1}(X; \mathbb{Z}), \mathbb{Q}) = H_k(X; \mathbb{Z}) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Q}.\] Hence in the Whitehead tower we can apply Hurewicz to \(\tau_{\geq n} X\) as usual and obtain \[\pi_n(\tau_{\geq n} X) \simeq H_n(\tau_{\geq n} X; \mathbb{Z}),\] then tensor with \(\mathbb{Q}\) and apply the previous isomorphism from UCT to get \[\pi_n(\tau_{\geq n} X) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Q}\simeq H_n(\tau_{\geq n} X; \mathbb{Z}) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Q}\simeq H_n(\tau_{\geq n} X; \mathbb{Q}).\]
I’ll also mention a nice result which we don’t need here but can be proven by some contemplation of the Atiyah–Hirzebruch spectral sequence plus classical Hurewicz:

Theorem 3 (Rational Hurewicz) Assume \(X\) is a simply connected space with \(\pi_k(X) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Q}= 0\) for all \(0 < k < n\). Then the Hurewicz map induces an isomorphism \[\pi_k(X) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Q}\xrightarrow{\sim} H_k(X; \mathbb{Q})\] for all \(0 < k < 2n-1\) and a surjection for \(k = 2n-1\).

So we’ve reduced Serre’s theorem to computing some rational homology groups, which is very helpful because \(\mathbb{Q}\)-vector spaces have much more structure than \(\mathbb{Z}\)-modules. However, our main tool for computing homology – the Serre spectral sequence – computes integral (co)homology. Luckily this is not a big deal; we just need to make a small modification to the Serre spectral sequence.
Suppose we’re in the setup of the usual Serre spectral sequence; i.e. we have a fibre sequence \[F \to E \to B\] where \(B\) is simply connected. For any abelian group \(A\) we have the Atiyah–Hirzebruch spectral sequences1 \[\begin{align*}
E_2^{p,q} = H^p(B; H^q(F; A)) &\Rightarrow H^{p+q}(E; A)\\
E^2_{p,q} = H_p(B; H_q(F; A)) &\Rightarrow H_{p+q}(E; A).
\end{align*}\] We might generally expect that with \(A = \mathbb{Q}\) or any field, computations with the Atiyah–Hirzebruch spectral sequence should be easier than the Serre spectral sequence, since all extension problems that arise will be trivial.
With rational Hurewicz and Atiyah–Hirzebruch in our pocket, we can start computing some homology. However, as with the computation of \(\pi_4(S^3)\) using the Serre spectral sequence, it will be easier in general to compute cohomology first and then invoke the UCT.

title: Serre’s Finiteness Theorem (Homotopy Groups of Spheres Part II)
text:
Cohomology of Eilenberg–MacLane Spaces
Since Eilenberg–MacLane spaces are the fibres of each stage in the Whitehead tower, we need to know their rational cohomology to apply the Atiyah–Hirzebruch spectral sequence to inductively compute rational cohomology of \(\tau_{\geq k} S^n\).

Lemma 1 We have \[H^*(K(\mathbb{Z},n); \mathbb{Q}) \simeq \begin{cases}
\mathbb{Q}[\alpha]/(\alpha^2) & \text{if } n \text{ is odd}\\
\mathbb{Q}[\alpha] & \text{if } n \text{ is even},
\end{cases}\] with \(|\alpha| = n\) in all cases.

As mentioned, I won’t write out the full details of this and subsequent proofs since it’s basically the same spectral sequence techniques each time. For this one, use Atiyah–Hirzebruch on the fibre sequence \[K(\mathbb{Z},n) \to * \to K(\mathbb{Z},n+1)\] to induct on \(n\), with the base cases \(n = 1\) and \(n = 2\) given by the fact that \(S^1 \simeq K(\mathbb{Z}, 1)\) and \(\mathbb{C}\mathbb{P}^\infty \simeq K(\mathbb{Z}, 2)\).
We’ll now start expanding from \(G = \mathbb{Z}\) to eventually get the rational cohomology of \(K(G,n)\) for any finitely generated abelian group \(G\). Our full statement is the following:

Proposition 1 Let \(G\) be a finitely generated abelian group; write \(G \simeq \mathbb{Z}^k \oplus A\) with \(A\) finite. Then \[H^*(K(G, n); \mathbb{Q}) \simeq \begin{cases}
\mathbb{Q}[\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_k]/(\alpha_1^2, \ldots, \alpha_k^2) & \text{if } n \text{ is odd}\\
\mathbb{Q}[\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_k] & \text{if } n \text{ is even},
\end{cases}\] with \(|\alpha_i| = n\) for all \(n\) and all \(i\).


Proof. Using Künneth, it suffices to show this statement for the case where \(G\) is cyclic. We already have the result for \(G \simeq \mathbb{Z}\), so we can assume \(G \simeq \mathbb{Z}/m\mathbb{Z}\) for some \(m > 1\). We now induct on \(n\); once we establish the base case \(n = 1\) it will be routine to apply Atiyah–Hirzebruch on the usual fibre sequence \[K(\mathbb{Z}/m, n) \to * \to K(\mathbb{Z}/m, n+1).\] For \(n = 1\), we can model \(K(\mathbb{Z}/m, 1)\) by the lens space \(L(\infty, m)\), which is the quotient of \(S^\infty\) by the \(\mathbb{Z}/m\)-action given by multiplication by \(m^{\text{th}}\) roots of unity. We could try to use Atiyah–Hirzebruch on the fibre sequence \[\mathbb{Z}/m \to S^\infty \to L(\infty, m),\] but this presents the following complications:

The spectral sequence doesn’t directly compute \(H^*(L(\infty, m); \mathbb{Q})\)
\(L(\infty, m)\) is not simply connected, so we would have to treat \(H^q(\mathbb{Z}/m; \mathbb{Q})\) as a local system on \(L(\infty, m)\) rather than a regular coefficient group.

We instead remember that the \(\mathbb{Z}/m\)-action on \(S^\infty\) comes from an action of \(S^1\), whose quotient gives \(\mathbb{C}\mathbb{P}^\infty\). This descends to an \(S^1\)-action on \(L(\infty, m)\), and quotienting by that action gives us the fibre sequence \[S^1 \to L(\infty, m) \to \mathbb{C}\mathbb{P}^\infty.\] From here, Atiyah–Hirzebruch easily gives that \(H^*(L(\infty, m); \mathbb{Q}) = \mathbb{Q}\) as desired.

title: Serre’s Finiteness Theorem (Homotopy Groups of Spheres Part II)
text:
Rational Homotopy of Spheres
With Eilenberg–MacLane spaces taken care of, we can now start attacking the Whitehead towers of spheres to get Serre’s theorem. The case where \(n\) is odd is the slightly simpler one. Starting with the fibre sequence \[K(\mathbb{Z}, n-1) \to \tau_{\geq n+1}S^n \to S^n,\] the usual method applied to Atiyah–Hirzebruch shows that the spectral sequence degenerates at \(E_{n+1}\) and all maps on the \(E_n\)-page are isomorphisms. Hence \(H^*(\tau_{\geq n+1} S^n;\mathbb{Q}) \simeq \mathbb{Q}\), and by UCT we also get that \(H_k(\tau_{\geq n+1}S^n; \mathbb{Q}) = 0\) for \(k > 0\). As discussed at the beginning, this establishes via rational Hurewicz that \(\pi_{n+1}S^n\) is finite. Now we can inductively show that \(\pi_{n+k}S^n\) is finite for \(k > 0\) by using Atiyah–Hirzebruch on the fibre sequence \[K(\pi_{n+k}S^n, n+k-1) \to \tau_{\geq n+k+1} S^n \to \tau_{\geq n+k} S^n.\] The finiteness of \(\pi_{n+k}S^n\) along with Proposition 1 gives \[H^*(K(\pi_{n+k}S^n, n+k-1); \mathbb{Q}) = \mathbb{Q}\] and we also inductively assume that \[H^*(\tau_{\geq n+k}S^n; \mathbb{Q}) = \mathbb{Q}.\] Then the \(E_2\)-page of the Atiyah–Hirzebruch spectral sequence has a single \(\mathbb{Q}\) at \(E_2^{0,0}\) and \(0\) everywhere else, so we instantly get \[H^*(\tau_{\geq n+k+1}S^n; \mathbb{Q}) = \mathbb{Q}.\] Applying UCT and Rational Hurewicz then yields finiteness of \(\pi_{n+k+1}S^n\).
Now for the case where \(n\) is even – we will eventually do the same induction as in the odd case, but to start off we need to do something different in lower degrees2. Start again with the fibre sequence \[K(\mathbb{Z}, n-1) \to \tau_{\geq n+1}S^n \to S^n\] and apply Atiyah–Hirzebruch. In this case the spectral sequence again degenerates at \(E_{n+1}\), but now we do not get all cohomology vanishing; instead we obtain a single nontrivial cohomology group in degree \(2n-1\) and thus \[H^*(\tau_{\geq n+1} S^n; \mathbb{Q}) \simeq \mathbb{Q}[\beta]/(\beta^2), \qquad |\beta| = 2n-1.\] If \(n > 2\) (which we can safely assume thanks to the Hopf map and the case of \(S^3\) already being done), we see as before that \[\pi_{n+1}(S^n) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Q}\simeq H_{n+1}(\tau_{\geq n+1}S^n; \mathbb{Q}) = 0,\] so \(\pi_{n+1}(S^n)\) is finite. Continuing with the fibre sequence \[K(\pi_{n+1}S^n, n) \to \tau_{\geq n+2}S^n \to \tau_{\geq n+1} S^n,\] the \(E_2\)-page of Atiyah–Hirzebruch is just a line – it has \(\mathbb{Q}\) at \(E_2^{0,0}\) and \(E_2^{2n-1,0}\) and \(0\) everywhere else. Hence we immediately see that again, \[H^*(\tau_{\geq n+2} S^n; \mathbb{Q}) \simeq \mathbb{Q}[\beta]/(\beta^2), \qquad |\beta| = 2n-1,\] and \(\pi_{n+2}(S^n)\) is finite. This argument repeats to give finiteness of \(\pi_{n+k}(S^n)\) for \(k < n-1\); at \(k = n-1\) we once again apply Atiyah–Hirzebruch to \[K(\pi_{2n-2}S^n, 2n-3) \to \tau_{\geq 2n-1}S^n \to \tau_{\geq 2n-2} S^n\] and obtain the same line on \(E_2\), with \(\mathbb{Q}\) at \(E_2^{0,0}\) and \(E_2^{2n-1,0}\). Once again, we find that \[H^*(\tau_{\geq 2n-1} S^n; \mathbb{Q}) \simeq \mathbb{Q}[\beta]/(\beta^2), \qquad |\beta| = 2n-1,\] but now we are interested in the degree \(2n-1\) homology. Hence we obtain now that \[\pi_{2n-1}(S^n) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Q}\simeq H_{2n-1}(\tau_{\geq 2n-1}S^n; \mathbb{Q}) \simeq \mathbb{Q},\] establishing the exceptional case in Serre’s theorem. From here we can repeat the same induction from the odd case (mutatis mutandis) to establish finiteness of \(\pi_{k}(S^n)\) for \(k > 2n-1\), as \(2n-1\) is now odd and \(\pi_{2n-1}(S^n)\) has rank \(1\).

title: Serre’s Finiteness Theorem (Homotopy Groups of Spheres Part II)
text:
Footnotes


More generally, we will have such spectral sequences for any generalized (co)homology theory.↩︎
as evidenced by the part of Serre’s theorem that \(\pi_{2n-1}(S^n)\) for even \(n\) is an exceptional case that is not finite↩︎

title: Homotopy Groups of Spheres
text:
It’s been way too long since I’ve made a blog post but hopefully I’ll be a little more regular with it now. I just finished a course on homotopy theory and I decided it was a good time to try computing some homotopy groups of spheres. Well, at least one homotopy group of a sphere. I’ll explain some constructions and results from homotopy theory and how to use them to compute the homotopy group \(\pi_4(S^3)\). This all ends up pointing towards a theorem of Serre which tells us that most homotopy groups of spheres are finite, and describes the few exceptions. I’ll probably make another post describing how to prove that result later.
Note: everything I talk about will be homotopy-theoretic; that means all spaces and maps should be understood as CW/Kan complexes up to homotopy or simplicial sets/topological spaces up to weak equivalence.

title: Homotopy Groups of Spheres
text:
Preliminary results from homotopy theory
I’ll start with some easier results about homotopy groups of spheres which aren’t necessarily needed to compute \(\pi_4(S^3)\) but give context for what we already know and can deduce from our computation.

\(S^0\) is just two points, so \(\pi_0(S^0) \simeq \mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\) (as pointed sets) and \(\pi_k(S^0) = 0\) for \(k > 0\).
We can compute \(\pi_k(S^1)\) easily by looking at its universal cover \(\mathbb{R}\twoheadrightarrow S^1\). We get \(\pi_0(S^1) = 0\), \(\pi_1(S^1) \simeq \mathbb{Z}\), and since \(\mathbb{R}\) is contractible \(\pi_k(S^1) \simeq \pi_k(\mathbb{R}) = 0\) for \(k > 2\).
The cellular approximation theorem tells us that for the purposes of homotopy groups we might as well assume all maps \(S^k \to S^n\) are cellular1. We can then conclude that \(\pi_k(S^n) = 0\) for \(k < n\).
The Hurewicz theorem says if \(\pi_k(X) = 0\) for all \(k < n\) then we have an isomorphism between homotopy and homology \(\pi_n(X) \xrightarrow{\sim} H_n(X)\). Hence we obtain that \(\pi_n(S^n) \simeq H_n(S^n) \simeq \mathbb{Z}\).
The Hopf map \(\eta \colon S^3 \to S^2\) is a fibration with fiber \(S^1\), hence the long exact sequence on homotopy groups concludes that \(\pi_k(S^3) \simeq \pi_k(S^2)\) for \(k \geq 3\). In particular, \(\pi_3(S^2) \simeq \mathbb{Z}\) and our computation of \(\pi_4(S^3)\) will give us \(\pi_4(S^2)\) for free.
The Freudenthal suspension theorem says the map \(S^n \to \Omega S^{n+1}\) induces an isomorphism \(\pi_k(S^n) \xrightarrow{\sim} \pi_{k+1}(S^{n+1})\) when \(k \leq 2n-2\). Hence we also get \(\pi_{n+1}(S^n)\) for free with our computation of \(\pi_4(S^3)\).

title: Homotopy Groups of Spheres
text:
Tools

The Whitehead tower
The first of our two main tools for computing homotopy groups of spheres is the Whitehead tower. Roughly, it’s a way to (homotopy-theoretically) cover a space by spaces which get closer and closer to a point. More precisely, for a space \(X\) there is a sequence of spaces \[\cdots \to \tau_{\geq 3}X \to \tau_{\geq 2}X \to \tau_{\geq 1}X \to \tau_{\geq 0}X = X\] with the property that

\(\pi_k(\tau_{\geq n}X) = 0\) for \(k < n\);
The map \(\tau_{\geq n}X \to X\) induces isomorphisms \(\pi_k(\tau_{\geq n}X) \xrightarrow{\sim} \pi_k(X)\) for \(k \geq n\).

So essentially we are killing off homotopy groups of \(X\) one by one – \(\tau_{\geq 2}X\) is actually the universal cover of \(X\) and the Whitehead tower may be thought of as a generalization of that to higher connectivity. This tower also has the property that we have “fibre sequences” \[K(\pi_n(X), n-1) \to \tau_{\geq n+1}X \to \tau_{\geq n}X;\] this roughly means that (homotopy-theoretically) the fibres of the map \(\tau_{\geq n+1}X \to \tau_{\geq n}X\) are these Eilenberg–Maclane spaces \(K(\pi_n(X), n-1)\), which are defined by the property that their only nonzero homotopy group is \(\pi_{n-1}(K(\pi_n(X), n-1)) \simeq \pi_n(X)\). What this does is it gives us a way to think of \(X\) as being built out of spaces which are very simple from the POV of homotopy theory.


The Serre spectral sequence
Our other main character is the Serre spectral sequence (there’s actually two of them but they’re kind of the same thing). If we have a fibre sequence \[F \to E \to B\] where \(B\) is simply connected, then we get two spectral sequences relating the (co)homology of \(E\) to that of \(F\) and \(B\): \[\begin{align*}
E_2^{p,q} = H^p(B; H^q(F)) &\Rightarrow H^{p+q}(E)\\
E^2_{p,q} = H_p(B; H_q(F)) &\Rightarrow H_{p+q}(E).
\end{align*}\] I won’t go into what a spectral sequence is, but it’s basically a bookkeeping machine that helps us relate the groups \(H^{p+q}(E)\) to the groups \(H^p(B; H^q(F))\) via taking subgroups, quotients, and group extensions. As those familiar with spectral sequences might guess, the Serre spectral sequences have cohomological and homological Serre grading, respectively. Given the fibre sequences that come out of the Whitehead tower, you might guess where this goes: we can iteratively use the Serre spectral sequence(s) to compute (co)homology of \(\tau_{\geq n+1}X\) in terms of the (co)homology of \(\tau_{\geq n}X\) and \(K(\pi_n(X), n-1)\). There are a couple problems with this –

we wanted to compute homotopy groups, not (co)homology;
Eilenberg–Maclane spaces may have simple homotopy groups, but their (co)homology can get kinda complicated.

For a), we can bridge the gap between homotopy and homology using the Hurewicz theorem – the defining characteristics of the homotopy groups of \(\tau_{\geq n}X\) tell us that \[\pi_n(X) \simeq \pi_n(\tau_{\geq n}X) \simeq H_n(\tau_{\geq n}X).\] For b), we can try to iteratively understand the (co)homology of Eilenberg–Maclane spaces by applying Serre spectral sequences to the fibre sequence that comes from the Whitehead tower of \(K(A,n)\): \[K(A, n-1) \to * \to K(A,n).\]

title: Homotopy Groups of Spheres
text:
Finite generation of homotopy groups
Before actually computing \(\pi_4(S^3)\), we (rather annoyingly) need a couple lemmas about finite generation of homology/homotopy groups. Specifically:

Lemma 1 Let \(A\) be a finitely generated abelian group and \(n \in \mathbb{Z}\) positive. Then \(H_k(K(A,n))\) and \(H^k(K(A,n))\) are finitely generated for all \(k \geq 0\).


Lemma 2 Let \(X\) be a finite simply connected CW complex. The homotopy groups \(\pi_k(X)\) are finitely generated for all \(k \geq 0\).


Proof (Sketch for Lemma 1). 

Use the Künneth theorem to reduce to the case that \(A\) is cyclic and use UCT to reduce to proving this for homology.
For \(n = 1\), we can model \(K(\mathbb{Z}, 1)\) by \(S^1\) and \(K(\mathbb{Z}/n, 1)\) by an infinite dimensional lens space (a quotient of \(S^\infty\)). Both are CW complexes with finitely many cells in each dimension so we are done.
For \(n > 1\), we induct on \(n\) using the Serre spectral sequence on the fibre sequence \[K(A, n-1) \to * \to K(A, n).\] Since finitely generated abelian groups are closed under subgroups, quotients, and extensions2, we can track through the spectral sequence to find that finite generation of \(H_*(K(A,n-1))\) must imply finite generation of \(H_*(K(A,n))\).



Proof (Sketch for Lemma 2). 

As noted before, we have \(\pi_k(X) \simeq H_k(\tau_{\geq k}X)\) so it is enough to prove that \(\tau_{\geq k}X\) has finitely generated homology.
The simply connected assumption means \(\tau_{\geq 2}X = X\) so we have a base case.
We induct on \(k\) by using the fibre sequence \[K(\pi_k(X), k-1) \to \tau_{\geq k+1}X \to \tau_{\geq k}X\] and applying the Serre spectral sequence3.
By the first lemma we know \(K(\pi_k(X), k-1)\) has finitely generated homology so by our induction hypothesis and the fact that finitely generated abelian groups form a Serre class, we obtain that the homology of \(\tau_{\geq k+1}X\) is finitely generated.

title: Homotopy Groups of Spheres
text:
Computing \(\pi_4(S^3)\)
Now we are ready to compute \(\pi_4(S^3)\); we use the same trick with the Whitehead tower mentioned before that \(\pi_4(S^3) \simeq H_4(\tau_{\geq 4}S^3)\). To compute homology in this case, we use the fibre sequence \[K(\mathbb{Z}, 2) \to \tau_{\geq 4}S^3 \to S^3\] and take advantage of the fact that we know fairly well the two outer spaces. Homology of \(S^3\) is easy as it is a sphere, and we can model \(K(\mathbb{Z},2)\) by the infinite complex projective space \(\mathbb{CP}^\infty\). Even though we completely know these homologies, it turns out directly computing homology of \(\tau_{\geq 4}S^3\) through the Serre spectral sequence is hard, so we compute cohomology instead. This turns out to be easier since cohomology has a ring structure and the cohomological Serre spectral sequence is “multiplicative” (it respects the cup product in a suitable sense). Since \[H^n(S^3) \simeq \begin{cases}
\mathbb{Z}& n = 0,3\\
0 & \text{else}
\end{cases} \quad \text{and} \quad H^n(\mathbb{C}\mathbb{P}^\infty) \simeq \begin{cases}
\mathbb{Z}& n \equiv 0 \bmod 2\\
0 & n \equiv 1 \bmod 2
\end{cases},\] the \(E_2\) page of the cohomological Serre spectral sequence is  
The differentials on this page are all \(0\), so the groups remain the same on the \(E_3\) page. We get potentially nontrivial differentials on this page:   and we also see that the spectral sequence will degenerate on the \(E_4\) page. Moreover, every diagonal has at most one non-trivial group so the cohomology of \(\tau_{\geq 4}S^3\) can be read off directly from the \(E_4\) page. How do we determine what’s actually on the \(E_4\) page though??
We know already from Hurewicz and the UCT that \(\tau_{\geq 4}S^3\) has no cohomology in degrees \(1,2,3\). This tells us that the groups on those diagonals in \(E_4\) must vanish. So we get an isomorphism \(E_3^{0,2} \xrightarrow{\sim} E_3^{3,0}\), and all maps on the \(E_2\) page were \(0\) so going through definitions we have an isomorphism \[\mathbb{Z}\simeq H^2(\mathbb{CP}^\infty) \xrightarrow{\sim} H^3(S^3) \simeq \mathbb{Z}.\] Here’s where we take advantage of the fact that we’re looking at cohomology: the differentials in the spectral sequence satisfy a Leibniz rule with the cup product, which means that if \(\alpha\) is a generator of \(H^2(\mathbb{CP}^\infty)\), we have \[d_3(\alpha^2) = 2\alpha d_3(\alpha).\] In particular, the map \(\mathbb{Z}\simeq E_3^{0,4} \to E_3^{3,2} \simeq \mathbb{Z}\) is multiplication by \(2\). We can actually inductively show this way that the map \(\mathbb{Z}\simeq E_3^{0,2n} \to E_3^{3, 2n-2} \simeq \mathbb{Z}\) is multiplication by \(n\). This gives us the following computation for the cohomology of \(\tau_{\geq 4}S^3\): \[H^k(\tau_{\geq 4}, S^3) \simeq \begin{cases}
\mathbb{Z}& k = 0\\
0 & k = 1\\
0 & k = 2n > 0\\
\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}& k = 2n+1 > 1.
\end{cases}\]
We wanted homology though, and here’s where those two finite generation lemmas (Lemma 2) come in4: UCT tells us that \[0 = H^4(\tau_{\geq 4}S^3) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb{Z}}(H_4(\tau_{\geq 4}S^3), \mathbb{Z})\] and \[\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\simeq H^5(\tau_{\geq 4}S^3) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb{Z}}(H_5(\tau_{\geq 4}S^3), \mathbb{Z}) \oplus \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathbb{Z}}^1(H_4(\tau_{\geq 4}S^3), \mathbb{Z}).\] Since we know the homology of \(\tau_{\geq 4}S^3\) is finitely generated, we see from the first isomorphism that \(H_4(\tau_{\geq 4}S^3)\) is finite and from the second that it must be isomorphic to \(\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\), and at last we are done!

title: Homotopy Groups of Spheres
text:
Outlook
This process is quite technically involved, but theoretically it can be repeated to compute homotopy groups of any space. In general we may not have so nice a space modelling \(K(\pi_n(X), n-1)\), but as we’ve seen we can try to inductively use the Serre spectral sequence to understand homology of those spaces. In fact, working along these lines, Serre proved a much stronger version of the result that \(\pi_k(S^n)\) is always finitely generated:

Theorem 1 (Serre) \(\pi_k(S^n)\) is always finite, except in two cases:

If \(k = n\) then \(\pi_n(S^n) \simeq \mathbb{Z}\);
If \(n\) is even then \(\pi_{2n-1}(S^n) \simeq \mathbb{Z}\oplus A\) for some finite abelian group \(A\).


That’s something for another time though.

title: Homotopy Groups of Spheres
text:
Footnotes


A map of CW complexes which carries the \(m\)-skeleton into the \(m\) skeleton for all \(m\)↩︎
finitely generated abelian groups form a “Serre subcategory” or “Serre class” of abelian groups↩︎
we need \(X\) to be simply connected so we can start using the Serre spectral sequence at \(k = 2\)↩︎
We actually only need to know that \(H_4(\tau_{\geq 4}S^3)\) is finitely generated, so we could easily get this specific case from the homological Serre spectral sequence. The \(E^2\) page looks the same as the \(E_2\) page of the cohomological Serre spectral sequence, with differentials going the opposite direction. We again would find that the differentials on the \(E^2\) page are zero, so the same groups appear on the \(E^3\) page, and the spectral sequence degenerates on the \(E^4\) page. We can read off from this that \(E^4_{0,4} \simeq H_4(\tau_{\geq 4}S^3)\) is cyclic and thus finitely generated. So we can actually get most of the information of what \(\pi_4(S^3)\) is from the homological Serre spectral sequence, but we need the cup product structure on cohomology to pin down the order of the group.↩︎

title: About
text: This blog is a place for me to share (mathematical) things that have caught my interest, be they random musings or topics that I am actively learning about. My hope is that anyone who reads these posts will be able to learn something about the mathematics from my perspective.

title: A “proof” that primes are not square
text: Welcome to my blog! My aim is to post about interesting things that capture my attention, be they topics I am currently learning or random musings that I have. My hope is that whoever reads my posts finds them insightful and worthwhile. This first post is mostly a humorous argument1 that prime numbers are not square, though there are some connections to important concepts in algebraic number theory which I will highlight at the end.

title: A “proof” that primes are not square
text:
“Proof” that primes are not square
Let \(l\) be an arbitrary prime number. In order to show \(l\) is not a square integer, our goal will be to construct a number field \(K\) in which squares are local norms everywhere i.e. for every prime \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(K\), the square integers are in the norm group \(\mathrm{Norm}_{K_{\mathfrak{p}}/\mathbb{Q}_p}(K_{\mathfrak{p}}^\times)\). If we can construct such a \(K\) with the additional property that \(l\) is not a local norm at some place2, then we know that \(l\) cannot be a square. To start off, we can use the Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT) and Dirichlet’s Theorem on primes in arithmetic progressions to choose a prime \(p\) which is congruent to \(1 \bmod 4\) such that \(p \not\equiv\square \bmod l\) (\(p\) is not a square modulo \(l\)). Similarly, we can use CRT and Dirichlet’s theorem to choose a prime \(q\) which is different from \(p\), satisfying \(q \equiv 1 \bmod 4\) and \(q \equiv \square \bmod p\).
Our choice of \(p\) and \(q\) enforces that \(K = \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{p},\sqrt{q})\) satifies the following:

As \(p,q \equiv 1 \bmod 4\), the only primes ramifying in \(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{p})\) and \(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{q})\) are \(p\) and \(q\), respectively. Thus \(p\) and \(q\) are the only primes which ramify in \(K\).
Since \(q \equiv \square \bmod p\), we know that \(p\) splits in \(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{q})\). Since each prime is \(1 \bmod 4\) the law of quadratic reciprocity implies that \(p \equiv \square \bmod q\). So \(q\) also splits in \(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{p})\).
No prime can be completely inert in \(K\), as \(C_2 \times C_2\) cannot surject onto \(C_4\). Thus every local extension \(K_{\mathfrak{r}}/\mathbb{Q}_r\) has degree \(1\) or \(2\) as the ramified primes (\(p\) and \(q\)) have degree \(2\) local extensions.
All rational squares are local norms everywhere: if \(K_{\mathfrak{r}}/\mathbb{Q}_r\) has degree \(1\) then \(x^2\) is the norm of \(x^2\). Otherwise \(K_{\mathfrak{r}}/\mathbb{Q}_r\) is degree \(2\), in which case \(x^2\) is the norm of \(x\).
Since \(p \not\equiv\square \bmod l\) and \(p \equiv 1 \bmod 4\), the law of quadratic reciprocity gives that \(l \not\equiv\square \bmod p\). This means \(l\) is inert in \(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{p})\) and thus in \(K\) as well. Per the previous points, the extension \(K_{\mathfrak{l}}/\mathbb{Q}_l\) must have degree \(2\) so the norm image is \(l^{2\mathbb{Z}} \times \mathbb{Z}_l^\times\). This means \(l\) is not a local norm at itself.

As desired, we have constructed an extension \(K/\mathbb{Q}\) in which all squares are local norms everywhere but \(l\) is not a local norm at itself. Thus \(l\) cannot be a square, and since \(l\) was arbitrary we have shown that no prime is a square!

title: A “proof” that primes are not square
section: Serious Discussion: the Hasse Norm Theorem
text:
Serious Discussion: the Hasse Norm Theorem
The inspiration for this argument came from a construction in algebraic number theory relating to the following theorem:

Theorem 1 (Hasse Norm Theorem) Let \(L/K\) be a cyclic extension of number fields. Then \(\alpha \in K^\times\) is in the norm image of \(L^\times\) if and only if \(\alpha\) is in the local norm image \(\mathrm{Norm}_{L_{\mathfrak{q}}/K_{\mathfrak{p}}}(L_{\mathfrak{q}}^\times)\) for all primes \(\mathfrak{q}\) of \(L\).

This theorem is a consequence of global class field theory and is an important ingredient in the proof of the Hasse-Minkowski theorem. However, the Hasse Norm Theorem is true only for cyclic extensions: it fails even for the simplest class of non-cyclic abelian extensions – biquadratic extensions. We have already all but constructed a class of counterexamples:

Take a prime \(p \equiv 1 \bmod 4\) and choose another prime \(q \equiv 1 \bmod 4\) such that \(q \equiv \square \bmod p\). Set \(K = \mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{p}, \sqrt{q})\).
Per our earlier discussion, \(p\) and \(q\) are the only ramified primes in \(K\) and both split in \(K\) as well. Moreover every local extension has degree \(1\) or \(2\), so all squares are local norms everywhere.
Let \(S_p\) be the set of rational primes which split completely in \(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{p})\); for \(x \in \mathbb{Q}^\times\) we define the function \[\varphi(x) = \prod_{l \in S_p} (q,x)_l\] where \((a,b)_l\) is the Hilbert symbol. It turns out3 that \(\ker \varphi = \set{x \in \mathbb{Q}^\times : x^2 \in \mathrm{Norm}_{K/\mathbb{Q}}(K^\times)}\), and when \(x\) is divisible only by odd primes \(l\) such that \(\left(\frac{l}{p}\right) = -1\), we have \(\varphi(x) = \left(\frac{x}{q}\right)\).
Thus if (for example) \(l\) is any prime which is inert in both \(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{p})\) and \(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{q})\), then \(l^2\) will be a local norm everywhere but not a global norm.

title: A “proof” that primes are not square
text:
